questions.question: Under the Statute of Limitations 1957 (Ireland), what is the general limitation period for an action founded on a simple contract?

options.A: Three years from the date of breach
options.B: Six years from the date on which the cause of action accrued
options.C: Ten years from the date of the contract
options.D: Twelve years from the date of the contract

questions.explanation: Under section 11(1)(a) of the Statute of Limitations 1957 (Ireland), an action founded on a simple contract must be brought within six years from the date on which the cause of action accrued. The cause of action in contract generally accrues at the date of breach, not when loss is discovered. The six-year period is the standard limitation period applicable to most contractual claims in Ireland.

questions.question: For a contract executed as a deed (under seal) in Ireland, the limitation period under the Statute of Limitations 1957 is:

options.A: Six years from the date of breach
options.B: Ten years from the date of the deed
options.C: Twelve years from the date on which the cause of action accrued
options.D: Twenty years from the date on which the deed was signed

questions.explanation: Under section 11(5) of the Statute of Limitations 1957, an action upon a specialty (i.e., a contract executed as a deed/under seal) must be brought within twelve years from the date on which the cause of action accrued. This longer period reflects the greater formality and solemnity of deeds. The distinction between simple contracts (six years) and specialty contracts/deeds (twelve years) is a fundamental feature of Irish limitation law.

questions.question: When does the cause of action in a simple contract breach accrue for the purposes of the limitation period?

options.A: When the claimant discovers the breach
options.B: When the loss flowing from the breach is quantified
options.C: At the date of the breach itself, irrespective of when the claimant discovered the loss
options.D: When the claimant communicates their intention to sue

questions.explanation: In contract, the limitation period runs from the date of breach — not from when the claimant discovers the breach or when loss is first quantified. This is the accrual rule established by Limitation Act authorities and applicable in Ireland under the Statute of Limitations 1957. This can cause hardship in latent defect cases where the breach is not immediately apparent, which is why the legislature introduced certain modifications (e.g., for fraud and concealment under s.71 of the 1957 Act).

questions.question: Under section 71 of the Statute of Limitations 1957 (Ireland), the limitation period may be postponed where:

options.A: The defendant is a company rather than an individual
options.B: The action is based on fraud, or the right of action is concealed by the fraud of the defendant
options.C: The claimant is a consumer rather than a business
options.D: The contract relates to land rather than personal property

questions.explanation: Section 71 of the Statute of Limitations 1957 provides that where an action is based on fraud, or the right of action is concealed by the fraud of the defendant or their agent, the limitation period does not begin to run until the claimant has discovered the fraud or could with reasonable diligence have discovered it. This prevents defendants from taking advantage of limitation periods by concealing their wrongdoing. The section is analogous to s.32 of the Limitation Act 1980 in England.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the concept of 'acknowledgment' in the context of limitation of actions?

options.A: An acknowledgment occurs when the claimant serves formal legal proceedings on the defendant
options.B: An acknowledgment of a debt or liquidated claim by the defendant resets the limitation period, running afresh from the date of acknowledgment
options.C: An acknowledgment must be in a court-approved form to be effective
options.D: An acknowledgment by the debtor is effective only if witnessed by a solicitor

questions.explanation: Under section 58 of the Statute of Limitations 1957, if a person liable on a contract acknowledges the claim (or makes a part payment), the limitation period begins to run afresh from the date of the acknowledgment or payment. The acknowledgment must be in writing and signed by the person making it (or their agent). Crucially, it must contain a clear and unambiguous admission of the existing debt or liability — not merely an expression of regret or a promise to consider the matter.

questions.question: A claimant brings an action for breach of a simple contract seven years after the date of breach. The defendant pleads the Statute of Limitations 1957. The claimant argues the defendant fraudulently concealed the breach. In assessing this argument, which test applies?

options.A: Whether the claimant actually knew of the fraud before issuing proceedings
options.B: Whether the claimant could with reasonable diligence have discovered the concealment, and whether they in fact could not have done so earlier
options.C: Whether the defendant acted in bad faith generally
options.D: Whether the fraud rendered the contract voidable from inception

questions.explanation: Under section 71 of the Statute of Limitations 1957, the limitation period is postponed where the right of action is concealed by fraud until the claimant discovers the fraud or could with reasonable diligence have discovered it. The test is objective — not whether the claimant actually knew, but whether a person exercising reasonable diligence would have discovered the concealment. The claimant must show they could not, with reasonable diligence, have discovered the breach within the ordinary six-year period.

questions.question: The limitation period for an action for breach of contract affecting land (other than an action to recover the land itself) in Ireland is:
questions.explanation: An action for damages for breach of a contract affecting land (as opposed to an action to recover land itself, which carries a twelve-year period under the Statute of Limitations 1957) is generally treated as a simple contract action and is subject to the standard six-year limitation period under section 11(1)(a) of the 1957 Act. The twelve-year period applies to actions to recover land and actions on specialty (deeds), not to contractual damages claims relating to land.

questions.question: Under Irish limitation law, where the claimant was under a disability (e.g., a minor or a person of unsound mind) when the cause of action accrued, which of the following applies?

options.A: The limitation period runs normally from the date of breach regardless of the claimant's disability
options.B: The limitation period does not begin to run until the disability ceases or the claimant dies, whichever occurs first
options.C: The limitation period is reduced to three years where the claimant is a minor
options.D: The court has a discretion to extend the limitation period but it runs from the date of breach

questions.explanation: Under section 49 of the Statute of Limitations 1957, where a person is under a disability (including minority or unsoundness of mind) at the time the cause of action accrues, the limitation period does not run until the disability ceases or the claimant dies (whichever is the earlier). This protection ensures that vulnerable claimants are not disadvantaged by the running of time during the period of their incapacity. The period then runs afresh once the disability ends.

questions.question: An action to recover a debt or liquidated money demand under a simple contract in Ireland must be brought within:

options.A: Three years from the date the debt fell due
options.B: Six years from the date the debt fell due
options.C: Ten years from the date the debt fell due
options.D: Twelve years from the date the debt fell due

questions.explanation: An action to recover a debt under a simple contract is subject to the standard six-year limitation period under section 11(1)(a) of the Statute of Limitations 1957. The cause of action for recovery of a debt accrues when the debt falls due and payable. An acknowledgment or part payment under section 58 will reset this period. Debts secured by deed (specialties) benefit from the twelve-year period under section 11(5).

questions.question: The Statute of Limitations (Amendment) Act 1991 (Ireland) introduced which important modification to the general limitation rules?

options.A: It reduced the limitation period for personal injury claims from six years to two years
options.B: It introduced a 'date of knowledge' test for personal injury claims, running from when the claimant knew or ought to have known they had a cause of action
options.C: It extended the limitation period for claims against professionals to ten years
options.D: It abolished the limitation period for claims founded on fraud

questions.explanation: The Statute of Limitations (Amendment) Act 1991 introduced a 'date of knowledge' test for personal injury claims, replacing the strict accrual date. The limitation period runs from the date of knowledge — when the claimant knew or ought reasonably to have known that they had suffered significant injury attributable to the defendant's act or omission. This modification was significant for cases involving latent injuries where the claimant could not reasonably have known of the cause of action at the date of the actual breach or act.

questions.question: In the context of contractual limitation clauses (i.e., contractual provisions purporting to shorten the limitation period), Irish courts will:

options.A: Treat such clauses as automatically void as contrary to public policy
options.B: Give effect to such clauses if they are clearly expressed, subject to reasonableness review under applicable consumer protection legislation
options.C: Require court approval before such clauses can be enforced
options.D: Apply the Statute of Limitations 1957 as the exclusive regime regardless of contractual terms

questions.explanation: Contractual limitation clauses that shorten the statutory limitation period are not automatically void — they may be enforced as a matter of freedom of contract where clearly expressed and agreed by the parties. However, in consumer contracts, such clauses are subject to scrutiny under the European Communities (Unfair Terms in Consumer Contracts) Regulations 1995 (SI 27/1995) and related consumer protection legislation. In commercial contracts between businesses of equal bargaining power, such clauses are generally enforceable if sufficiently clear.

questions.question: The limitation period for an action on an anticipatory breach of contract in Ireland begins to run from:

options.A: The date the anticipatory breach was communicated to the innocent party
options.B: The date on which performance was originally due under the contract
options.C: The date on which the innocent party accepts the anticipatory breach as a repudiation
options.D: The date on which the innocent party issues legal proceedings

questions.explanation: Following the acceptance of an anticipatory breach as a repudiation, the contract is treated as discharged at the date of acceptance, and the cause of action for damages accrues at that point. If the innocent party elects to affirm the contract and wait until the performance date, the cause of action accrues only when the breach of the subsisting obligation occurs (i.e., at the performance date). The election made by the innocent party therefore determines when the limitation clock starts running.

questions.question: Which of the following actions has a different limitation period to a simple contract claim under the Statute of Limitations 1957?

options.A: An action for the price of goods sold under the Sale of Goods Act 1893
options.B: An action on a cheque or bill of exchange
options.C: An action on a contract executed as a deed (specialty)
options.D: An action for breach of an oral agreement

questions.explanation: An action on a specialty — a contract executed as a deed — is subject to a twelve-year limitation period under section 11(5) of the Statute of Limitations 1957, whereas a simple contract action (whether oral, written, or evidenced in writing) is subject to the standard six-year period under section 11(1)(a). Actions under the Sale of Goods Act for the price of goods, actions on bills of exchange, and actions for breach of oral agreements are all simple contract actions attracting the six-year period.

questions.question: An Irish court has jurisdiction to strike out a claim as statute-barred. When will a defendant typically raise a limitation defence?

options.A: At any time before judgment, including at the trial itself
options.B: Only by way of a formal application made within 28 days of service of proceedings
options.C: Limitation must be raised at the earliest opportunity or it is waived
options.D: The court will raise the limitation issue of its own motion in all cases

questions.explanation: Under Irish procedural rules, a limitation defence is typically pleaded as a special defence in the defence of the defendant or raised by way of a motion to strike out. It may be raised at any time before judgment, including at trial. Unlike jurisdictional issues, the court will not generally raise a limitation defence on its own motion — it is for the defendant to plead it. A defendant who fails to plead the limitation defence in their pleadings may be seen as having waived it, but the general position allows it to be raised throughout the proceedings.

questions.question: In the context of a continuing breach of contract, how is the limitation period calculated?

options.A: Time runs from the date of the first breach, regardless of how long the breach continues
options.B: A fresh cause of action arises each day or each period the breach continues, and time runs separately for each such cause of action
options.C: The limitation period is suspended for the duration of the continuing breach
options.D: The limitation period does not apply to continuing breaches — the claimant may sue at any time

questions.explanation: Where a breach of contract is continuing in nature — for example, a landlord's failure to repair premises or an ongoing failure to pay a periodic sum — a fresh cause of action arises continuously as the breach persists. The limitation period runs separately for each successive breach. A claimant may therefore recover for breaches occurring within six years of the date of issue, even if earlier breaches are time-barred. This principle prevents a defendant from completely barring a claim simply because the first breach occurred more than six years ago.

questions.question: In Ireland, what is the limitation period applicable to actions for breach of a construction contract where the claim is for latent defects?

options.A: The six-year period runs from the date of breach (when the defective work was carried out), regardless of when the defect became apparent
options.B: The six-year period runs from the date of practical completion in all cases
options.C: A special ten-year limitation period applies to all construction contracts
options.D: The limitation period is always extended by any defects liability period in the contract

questions.explanation: In Ireland, the cause of action for breach of a construction contract accrues at the date of breach — when the defective work was carried out, not when the defect was discovered. This application of the strict accrual rule (under the Statute of Limitations 1957) can be harsh for latent defects cases. The courts may apply the fraud and concealment provisions under s.71 of the 1957 Act in egregious cases, but there is no general 'date of knowledge' test for property/construction defect claims equivalent to that introduced for personal injury claims by the 1991 Act.

questions.question: Under Irish law, can parties to a commercial contract agree to extend the statutory limitation period beyond the time prescribed by the Statute of Limitations 1957?

options.A: No — parties cannot extend the statutory limitation period; only the legislature can do so
options.B: Yes — parties may contractually agree to extend or waive the limitation period, subject to any applicable statutory or public policy constraints
options.C: Yes, but only with the prior approval of the Irish courts
options.D: No — the Statute of Limitations 1957 expressly prohibits contractual extension of limitation periods

questions.explanation: In Irish law, parties to a commercial contract may agree to extend the limitation period or to waive the limitation defence. Freedom of contract allows parties to modify their mutual rights including the right to plead limitation. Courts have recognised that a contractual standstill agreement (by which parties agree not to plead limitation for a period) is enforceable. However, the position in consumer contracts is more restricted under consumer protection legislation, and any such clause would be subject to scrutiny for unfairness.

questions.question: An action for recovery of land under the Statute of Limitations 1957 must be brought within:

options.A: Six years from the date of dispossession
options.B: Ten years from the date of dispossession
options.C: Twelve years from the date on which the right to recover arose
options.D: Twenty years from the date on which the land was first occupied by the adverse possessor

questions.explanation: Section 13 of the Statute of Limitations 1957 provides that an action to recover land must be brought within twelve years from the date on which the right of action first accrued to the person bringing the action or to any person through whom they claim. After the twelve-year period, the title of the paper owner is extinguished under section 24 of the 1957 Act. This is the foundation of the doctrine of adverse possession in Irish land law and is significant in any contract law context involving the sale of or dispute over land.
